stem: An 82-year-old woman with Alzheimer disease has been on a maximally titrated, well-tolerated cholinesterase inhibitor for 18 months. Her MMSE has fallen from 19 to 11 and she now needs help with dressing and toileting; her cardiologist confirms no bradyarrhythmia and she has no GI complaints. The team wants to ADD a second agent whose mechanism is distinct from her current drug and that can be safely combined with it to target this moderate-to-severe stage. Which agent should be added?
explanation: She is already on a cholinesterase inhibitor and has progressed into the moderate-to-severe range. Memantine is an uncompetitive NMDA-receptor antagonist — a mechanism entirely distinct from the cholinesterase inhibitors — so it is the agent indicated for moderate-to-severe disease and the one that can be added ON TOP of an existing cholinesterase inhibitor without overlapping cholinergic toxicity. Donepezil, rivastigmine and galantamine are all cholinesterase inhibitors; adding another one alongside her current drug simply doubles cholinergic (GI, bradycardic) load through the same mechanism rather than providing a complementary one, and galantamine is in any case restricted to mild-to-moderate Alzheimer.
focus: NMDA antagonist for moderate-severe / add-on to a cholinesterase inhibitor

source.name: CANMAT/CCCDTD dementia pharmacotherapy — combination therapy and severity staging

stem: A 76-year-old man with mild-to-moderate Alzheimer disease needs a cholinesterase inhibitor, but oral cholinergic therapy has repeatedly caused prominent nausea and vomiting during titration, and his daughter reports he frequently forgets his pills and sometimes hides them. The team wants a cholinesterase inhibitor in a formulation that delivers smoother drug levels to reduce GI side effects and lets the caregiver confirm adherence at a glance. Which agent uniquely offers this?
explanation: Rivastigmine is the only agent here available as a transdermal patch, which produces smoother plasma levels than oral dosing, reduces the GI side effects that are limiting him, and lets the caregiver visually confirm the patch is on for adherence. Donepezil comes only as a tablet or orally-disintegrating tablet, galantamine only as an immediate-release tablet or extended-release capsule — neither has a patch. Memantine is not a cholinesterase inhibitor at all, comes only as a tablet, and is reserved for moderate-to-severe disease, so it does not fit this mild-to-moderate patient who needs cholinergic therapy in a better-tolerated delivery form.
focus: rivastigmine transdermal patch (GI tolerability and adherence)

source.name: Rivastigmine monograph — transdermal patch formulation and GI tolerability

stem: A 70-year-old man with newly diagnosed mild Alzheimer disease lives alone with no caregiver supervision and is a poor historian who admits he 'sometimes goes a few days' without taking any medication. His cardiac workup and renal function are normal. The team wants to start a symptomatic cognitive agent whose long half-life makes it once-daily and forgiving of the occasional missed dose, without the risk that an interruption forces a restart at a lower dose. Which agent best fits?
explanation: Donepezil has the longest half-life of the cholinesterase inhibitors, so it is dosed once daily and is forgiving of an occasional missed dose — ideal for an unsupervised patient with erratic adherence, and it is first-line across all stages including mild disease. Rivastigmine is the trap: after an interruption of more than about 3 days it must be restarted low and re-titrated, because resuming the prior dose risks severe vomiting — the opposite of forgiving. Galantamine has a shorter half-life, must be taken with food and fluids, and carries renal/hepatic dosing limits. Memantine is an NMDA antagonist reserved for moderate-to-severe disease and is not a first-line choice for this mild, treatment-naive patient.
focus: cholinesterase-inhibitor pharmacokinetics — donepezil long half-life and missed-dose tolerance vs rivastigmine re-titration

source.name: Donepezil and rivastigmine monographs — half-life, dosing frequency, and re-titration after interruption
